item.title: “By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence…
item.description:
“By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence is abandoned, all ignorance is abandoned, all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a farmer in autumn, using a large plow, cuts through all the roots while plowing — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a grass-cutter cuts down grass, takes it by the top, shakes it up and down, left and right, and then throws it away — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as when a bunch of mangoes is cut from its stalk, all the mangoes that were attached to the stalk are cut off with it — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.102
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Venerable Todeyya: “One in whom desires do not dwell, One in whom craving is not found, One who has crossed beyond doubt — What is…
item.description:
Venerable Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
What is the liberation of such a one?”

The Blessed One replied to Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
There is no other kind of liberation.”

Venerable Todeyya:
“Does such a one have longing,
Or is he free from longing?
Does he have wisdom,
Or is he still acquiring wisdom?
O all-seeing sage, explain this to me,
So that I may know what a sage is, O Sakyan.”

The Blessed One:
“He is free from longing,
Without any longing —
He is wise, not acquiring wisdom.
Thus, Todeyya, know the sage:
Possessing nothing,
Not attached to sensual existence.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.10
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Ānanda, he reflects: ‘Those low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble thoughts that are not connected with the goal and do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace,…
item.description:
“Ānanda, he reflects: ‘Those low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble thoughts that are not connected with the goal and do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna — such as sensual thoughts, ill-will thoughts, harmfulness thoughts — I will not think those thoughts.’ In this way, he is clearly aware of that.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 122 – Mahāsuññatasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated…
item.description:
“‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging? If his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging, then there is no arising, origination of suffering regarding future birth, aging, and death.’ Good sirs, how is consciousness distracted and scattered externally? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, consciousness pursues the sign of form, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of form, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of form—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, consciousness pursues the sign of phenomenon, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. Thus, good sirs, is consciousness distracted and scattered externally.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 138 – Uddesavibhaṅgasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp…
item.description:
“Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the eye, practice restraint of the eye. When the ear hears a sound … nose smells an odor … tongue tastes a flavor … body feels a touch … mind cognizes phenomena, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the mind, practice restraint of the mind.’”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 39 – Mahāhatthipadopamasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is…
item.description:
“Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that form changes and alters. When form changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that consciousness changes and alters. When consciousness changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. Thus, monks, is non-clinging and non-worry.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.8
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, devas and humans delight in form, are excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. They…
item.description:
“Monks, devas and humans delight in form, are excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. They delight in sound … in odor … in taste … in touch … Monks, devas and humans delight in phenomena, are excited by phenomena. When phenomena change, fade, and cease, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. Monks, the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One, having directly known as they actually are the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to form, does not delight in form, does not cherish form, is not excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, the Tathāgata dwells at ease. He does not delight in sound … in odor … in taste … in touch … Having directly known as they actually are the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to phenomena, the Tathāgata does not delight in phenomena, does not cherish them, is not excited by them. When phenomena change, fade, and cease, the Tathāgata dwells at ease.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.136
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Fear, suffering, and disease, A boil, and a dart, Bondage and mud, And also a womb — These are called sensual pleasures, Where worldlings become…
item.description:
Fear, suffering, and disease,
A boil, and a dart,
Bondage and mud,
And also a womb —
These are called sensual pleasures,
Where worldlings become attached,
Sinking into the beautiful,
They enter a womb again.
When a monk is ardent,
Not lax, mindful,
He crosses this dangerous path,
This path so hard to cross.
He sees beings living in fear,
Because of birth and aging,
Oppressed and conquered.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 8.56
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what is the burden? The five aggregates of clinging are the answer. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging,…
item.description:
“Monks, what is the burden? The five aggregates of clinging are the answer. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging, the perception aggregate of clinging, the formation aggregate of clinging, the consciousness aggregate of clinging. This, monks, is called the burden. And what, monks, is the bearer of the burden? The person — the venerable one of such a name and such a clan. This, monks, is called the bearer of the burden. And what, monks, is the taking up of the burden? It is this craving that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there — that is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, craving for non-existence. This, monks, is called the taking up of the burden. And what, monks, is the laying down of the burden? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance. This, monks, is called the laying down of the burden.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.22
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “This city is built of bones, Plastered with flesh and blood; Within dwells aging and death, Conceit and hypocrisy.” “Even the king’s splendid chariot wears…
item.description:
“This city is built of bones,
Plastered with flesh and blood;
Within dwells aging and death,
Conceit and hypocrisy.”

“Even the king’s splendid chariot wears out,
So too this body eventually ages.
But the Dhamma of the good does not age —
So the good indeed speak to the good.”

“A person of little learning,
Like an ox, grows old;
His flesh increases,
But not his wisdom.”

From: Dhammapada 146-156
📖 Read this sutta
